privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.subtitle: How we handle your data. Our core stance: every tool runs locally in your browser whenever possible — your content is never uploaded or retained.
privacy.body:
1. What we collect

OneTools is a collection of developer utilities. The following may be collected as you use the site:

  • Anonymous usage analytics: We use Google Analytics to log anonymous visit data (pageviews, dwell time, referrer) to improve the product. GA writes a small set of cookies.
  • Content you submit to AI tools: For features like JSON natural-language query and Markdown rewrite, your input is forwarded from our Vercel Edge Function to an LLM provider (currently Groq). We do not store this content on our servers, but the model provider may retain it briefly per their own policy (see below).
  • Locally-running tools: Image compression, background / watermark removal, timestamp conversion, Markdown preview — all run entirely in your browser. Nothing leaves your device.

2. What we do NOT collect

  • Account credentials (the site doesn't require signup)
  • Server-side copies of your images, documents or sensitive data
  • Any personally identifiable information (PII)

3. Third-party services

  • Google Analytics: anonymous traffic stats. See Google Privacy Policy.
  • Groq / OpenAI-compatible LLM providers: AI calls route through their API. See Groq Privacy Policy.
  • Hugging Face: On first use of the BG-removal / watermark tools, we download an AI model file from Hugging Face to your browser. They will see the HTTP request's IP and User-Agent.
  • Google AdSense (planned): We plan to display Google ads. Google will use cookies to personalize ads. Visit Google Ad Settings to manage preferences.
  • Vercel / jsDelivr / Google Fonts: CDNs for static assets and scripts. They will see your IP address.

4. Cookies

The following cookies may be set:

  • _ga / _ga_*: Google Analytics identifier.
  • onetools-lang: remembers your UI language. Local only, never uploaded.

5. Your rights

You may:

  • Clear browser cookies to reset your GA identifier;
  • Use your browser's privacy mode to visit the site;
  • Contact us via email (see About page) to request cessation of tracking.

6. Children's privacy

This site is not directed at children under 13. If you are a guardian and find a minor using the site, please contact us.

7. Changes to this policy

Material changes will be announced on the site. The latest version is marked by the "Last updated" date at the top of this page.

terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.subtitle: Please read before using OneTools. TL;DR: it's free, provided as-is, and you are responsible for lawful use.
terms.body:
1. The service

OneTools ("the site") is a set of online developer tools maintained by an individual developer, freely accessible to the public.

2. Acceptance of terms

Using the site constitutes agreement with these terms. If you do not agree, please stop using the site.

3. Prohibited use

You agree NOT to:

  • Use the site for any illegal activity (including but not limited to removing copyrighted watermarks from content you do not own, tampering with others' work);
  • Abusively automate requests, scan, or attack the site or the third-party APIs behind it;
  • Reverse engineer, decompile, or attempt to bypass site limits;
  • Embed the site into third-party apps in ways that mislead users or infringe on the brand.

4. Content responsibility

All content you process through the site (JSON, Markdown, images, etc.) is your own responsibility. The site does not review, back up, or warrant the legality of any content. In particular:

  • Use image tools only on material you own or have rights to;
  • AI-generated / rewritten text may be inaccurate — verify before use;
  • We are not liable for any loss (data loss, legal disputes, etc.) resulting from use of the site.

5. Service availability

The site is provided "as-is" with no guarantee of uninterrupted availability, AI feature availability, or accuracy of results. We may add, change, or discontinue features at any time.

6. Disclaimer

To the maximum extent permitted by law, we are not liable for any direct or indirect damages.

7. Intellectual property

The site's code is MIT-licensed (see the GitHub repo). The brand name and design belong to the site owner. Content you process on the site remains yours.

8. Changes to these terms

Terms may change as the service evolves. The latest version is marked by the "Last updated" date. Material changes will be announced on the site.

9. Governing law

These terms are governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China (excluding HK/MO/TW).

about.title: About OneTools
about.subtitle: Why this toolkit exists, and the principles behind it.
about.body:
What we make

OneTools is a browser-local developer toolkit for engineers and content creators. Compared to traditional "online tools" sites, our differentiators are:

  • Privacy first: Anything that can run locally, will. Image compression, background removal, watermark removal, Markdown preview, timestamp conversion — all of these run entirely inside your browser.
  • AI-augmented where it matters: For tasks that genuinely need semantic understanding (JSON natural-language query, Markdown rewrite), we route through a lightweight Edge Function to open-source LLMs for a fluid experience.
  • Open source and free: The code lives on GitHub — anyone can audit, contribute, or fork.

Stack

  • Frontend: React 18 + Vite + react-router
  • AI backend: Vercel Edge Functions (Node.js Runtime), calling OpenAI-compatible APIs (Groq by default)
  • Client-side AI: onnxruntime-web (inpainting), @imgly/background-removal, chrono-node (time parsing), marked (Markdown)

Tools

  • JSON formatter · AI query · Schema inference
  • Live Markdown preview · AI rewrite
  • Image compression (JPEG / WebP / PNG)
  • Timestamp ↔ date · natural-language parsing
  • AI background remover
  • AI watermark remover (local inference)

License

MIT — see GitHub repo. Issues and Pull Requests welcome.

Contact

For bugs, collaboration, or suggestions, please use GitHub Issues.

cmp.title: Compared to the usual alternatives
cmp.sub: Take a familiar job — remove a watermark, or produce an ID photo. Here's how the common routes look side by side.

idPhoto.q: Is it really free with no hidden paywall?
idPhoto.a: Yes. Image processing runs entirely in your browser via the @imgly/background-removal WASM model, so there are no server costs to recoup — no per-image fees, no premium tier.

idPhoto.q: Does my photo ever get uploaded?
idPhoto.a: Never. The full pipeline — cutout, background replacement, crop — runs locally via WebAssembly. You can even use it offline (first visit downloads a ~60 MB model, cached afterwards).
1-inch (295×413), 2-inch (413×579), small 1-inch (260×378), big 1-inch (390×567), US passport (600×600), China visa (390×567). Backgrounds: white / blue / red / gray + custom color picker.

idPhoto.q: Is the quality good enough for official use?
idPhoto.a: For visa applications, driver licenses, student ID, and résumé photos — absolutely. For passport photos needing physical-paper submission under strict official specs, we still recommend a studio (for pose/lighting standards).

idPhoto.q: Why is it slower than Remove.bg?
idPhoto.a: It runs on your device, not a GPU server. First load takes 30–60s to download the model; each photo then takes 3–8 seconds. Paid services are faster but require upload + payment.

removeWatermark.q: Is it as good as Photoshop?
removeWatermark.a: For regular text watermarks, small logos, and semi-transparent watermarks — very good. For complex textures or large occlusions, the LaMa model (~215 MB) may not be perfect; try to brush precisely over the watermark pixels only.

removeWatermark.q: Can I use this to remove copyrighted watermarks and then sell?
removeWatermark.a: No. Removing someone else's watermark may infringe intellectual property. This tool is only for content you own or have a license to edit. Use at your own responsibility.

removeWatermark.q: Why is the first run so slow?
removeWatermark.a: First use downloads the ~215 MB LaMa ONNX model from Hugging Face. The browser caches it — subsequent opens are nearly instant.
Not yet. Single image at a time. If you need bulk processing (100+ images), open a GitHub issue and we may add a queue mode.
Be precise. Cover only the watermark pixels with a small margin. Painting too broadly forces LaMa to regenerate large regions, which degrades quality visibly.

pdfSummary.a: The PDF itself is never uploaded. pdfjs-dist extracts plain text in your browser; only the text (not the PDF file) is sent to the LLM for summarization. Safe for non-confidential docs; for secret contracts, use your judgement.

Very accurate for structured documents (papers, contracts, annual reports, white papers). Scanned PDFs need to go through our OCR tool first. For dense legal clauses, treat AI summary as triage — do human verification on key clauses.
Text over ~30k characters is truncated before sending to the model (which covers most papers and contracts). For huge PDFs (100+ pages), use our PDF split tool first to batch them.

pdfSummary.q: Can I summarize a scanned PDF?
pdfSummary.a: Not directly — scanned pages have no text layer. Workflow: run the scan through OneTools' OCR first → paste the text into the summary tool.

pdfSummary.q: Will this become a paid feature?
pdfSummary.a: Free forever. LLM calls use free-tier open-source models (currently Llama 3.3 70B). If daily quota runs out there may be brief rate limits.

videoCompress.q: Can I get under 25 MB for chat / WeChat?
videoCompress.a: In most cases, yes. Pick the 'WeChat / chat' preset (720p, CRF 30). A 10-minute 1080p clip usually compresses to 15–25 MB. Very long videos: use our 'video to GIF' tool to snip the key moments instead.
No. All processing happens in your browser via FFmpeg.wasm. First use downloads a ~30 MB FFmpeg core; cached, works offline afterward.

videoCompress.q: Why is it slower than Handbrake / ffmpeg native?
videoCompress.a: It's single-threaded WebAssembly (multi-thread needs COOP/COEP headers which Vercel doesn't send by default). Roughly 1/3 the speed of native FFmpeg. 10-min video may take 3–5 minutes — but it's free, zero install, zero upload.
Input: MP4 / MOV / WebM / AVI / MKV / FLV / M4V. Output: MP4 (H.264 + AAC) for maximum compatibility.
We use CRF (constant quality) encoding, not crude bitrate limiting. The 'Balanced' and 'HD' presets are nearly indistinguishable. 'WeChat' is visibly compressed but acceptable.

fileEncrypt.q: What if I forget the password?
fileEncrypt.a: There is no recovery. This is standard AES-256-GCM + PBKDF2 (210k iterations) with no backdoor. Use a password manager (Bitwarden / 1Password). Forgetting the password = permanently lost file.

Yes. They open OneTools' file encrypt / decrypt tool → switch to 'Decrypt' → drop your .enc file → enter the password you share. Important: share the password over a separate, secure channel (not in the same email as the file).

fileEncrypt.q: How is this different from 7z / WinRAR encryption?
fileEncrypt.a: Similar algorithm (AES-256). Differences: (1) OneTools runs entirely in the browser, no install needed; (2) the .enc format is ours — recipient doesn't need any software, just their browser.

PBKDF2 at 210k iterations — brute-forcing a strong password would take billions of years at 2024 hardware rates. But a weak password (birthday / 123456) defeats any encryption. Use a manager to generate 16+ random characters.
Theoretically unlimited, in practice bounded by browser memory. Under 1 GB is fine on most devices; for huge files, split them or use professional desktop tools.
